Before Stage 1

?What Is AI, and How Did We Get to Agents?

A flowchart tells you the steps. It doesn't tell you why those steps exist, or why "agent" has become the word everyone uses in 2026. Before we get into building one, it's worth spending a few minutes on where this all came from — because almost every design choice in the eight stages that follow is really an answer to a problem someone ran into decades before ChatGPT existed.

What "artificial intelligence" actually means

At its plainest, artificial intelligence is the attempt to get a machine to do something that would normally require human judgement — recognising a face, translating a sentence, deciding the next best move, holding a conversation. That's a deliberately loose definition, and it's loose on purpose: what counted as "AI" in 1960 (a program that could prove basic logic theorems) is now considered ordinary software, because the goalposts move every time a capability becomes routine. This matters for a builder today because "AI" is not one technology. It is a decades-long accumulation of different techniques, each solving a different piece of the same problem: how do you get a machine to behave usefully in situations nobody explicitly programmed for?

The early decades: rules, logic, and the first winter (1950s–1980s)

The field formally began in 1956 at a summer workshop at Dartmouth College, where researchers set out with genuine optimism that machine intelligence comparable to a human's was maybe a decade away. The dominant approach for the next thirty years was symbolic AI: hand-written rules and logical statements, of the form "if this fact is true, then conclude that." Expert systems built this way could diagnose diseases or configure computer orders within a narrow domain, as long as every rule they'd ever need had been anticipated and typed in by a human expert. The approach worked well in closed, well-defined worlds and fell apart everywhere else — it had no way to handle a situation nobody had thought to write a rule for. Funding and enthusiasm collapsed twice, in the mid-1970s and again in the early 1990s, in periods researchers still call the "AI winters." The lesson that generation of researchers took away, whether they liked it or not, was that intelligence built entirely from hand-written rules doesn't generalise. Something else was needed.

Machine learning: teaching the rules to write themselves

Machine learning flipped the symbolic approach on its head. Instead of a person writing the rules, you show the machine thousands or millions of examples — emails marked spam or not spam, houses with their sale prices, X-rays with a doctor's diagnosis attached — and let a statistical algorithm work out the pattern for itself. This is the era that gave the world spam filters, credit-card fraud detection, and recommendation engines, through the 1990s and 2000s. It was a genuine leap forward, but it still had a ceiling: classic machine learning needed a human to decide which features of the data mattered (is it the sender's domain, the number of exclamation marks, the time of day?) before the algorithm could even start learning. Getting that "feature engineering" right was slow, specialised work, and it meant every new problem needed a human expert to hand-design the ingredients again.

Deep learning and neural networks: the machine learns the features too

Neural networks, loosely modelled on how neurons connect in a brain, had existed since the 1950s in simple forms, but they were too weak and too slow to train on anything meaningful. Three things changed that between roughly 2006 and 2012: far more data was suddenly available (the internet), far more computing power became affordable (GPUs, originally built for video games, turned out to be extremely good at the maths neural networks need), and researchers found better ways to train networks with many stacked layers — which is what "deep" in deep learning refers to. The breakthrough moment most historians point to is 2012, when a deep neural network called AlexNet dramatically beat every previous approach at recognising objects in photographs. What made deep learning different from everything before it was that the network learned its own features directly from raw data — pixels, sound waves, words — without a human hand-designing what to look for. That one shift is arguably the single most important idea behind every AI system you interact with today.

The transformer and the large language model (2017–2022)

In 2017, a team at Google published a paper with the almost cheeky title "Attention Is All You Need," introducing an architecture called the transformer. Its key trick, called self-attention, let a model weigh the relevance of every word in a passage against every other word simultaneously, instead of reading strictly left to right the way earlier neural networks did. This turned out to be extraordinarily good at language, and extraordinarily good at being trained in parallel on enormous amounts of text at once. Over the next five years, labs scaled this architecture up by orders of magnitude — more parameters, more training text scraped from books and the web, more compute — and something researchers still debate the full explanation for happened: capabilities that were never explicitly trained for began to emerge, including rudimentary reasoning, translation between languages the model was never specifically taught to translate, and the ability to follow instructions it had never seen phrased that exact way before. This is the technology behind GPT, Claude, Gemini, and every other large language model (LLM). When ChatGPT launched to the public in November 2022, it was less a sudden invention than the moment this decade of underlying research became something anyone could type into.

From chatbot to agent: giving the model hands, memory, and a job to do

A base LLM, on its own, is a very sophisticated autocomplete: give it text, it predicts what comes next. That's already useful for writing and answering questions, but it can't check today's weather, book a flight, update a spreadsheet, or remember what you told it last week once the conversation ends. Everything covered in the eight stages of this guide — tools, memory, orchestration, evals — is the engineering layer built around the model to close that gap. The industry calls the result an agent: an LLM that can decide which tool to call, act on the result, check its own work, and keep going across multiple steps toward a goal, rather than just answering one prompt and stopping. Early versions of this idea (AutoGPT and similar projects) appeared within months of ChatGPT's launch and were rough and unreliable. What's changed since then isn't a single breakthrough so much as the steady maturing of exactly the plumbing this guide walks through: better tool-calling built into the models themselves, standardised protocols like MCP so tools don't need custom integration for every model, vector databases and memory systems that actually hold up in production, and orchestration frameworks that can manage a multi-step plan without losing the thread.

Where this is headed

Nobody, including the labs building these systems, has a settled answer for how far agentic AI goes from here — and any specific prediction dates quickly, so it's worth treating forecasts (including this paragraph) with proportionate scepticism. What most practitioners agree is directionally true: agents are getting better at working over longer horizons with less supervision, multi-agent systems (several specialised agents coordinating on one task) are moving from research demos into production, and the boundary between "using an app" and "delegating a task to an agent" keeps blurring. What is very unlikely to change is the underlying shape of the problem this guide addresses: an agent is only as good as its scope, its instructions, its tools, its memory, and its testing — the five hard engineering questions this document was written to walk you through, one stage at a time. That's exactly where Stage 1 picks up.

This section summarises well-documented, publicly recorded history of AI research (Dartmouth 1956, expert systems and the AI winters, AlexNet in 2012, "Attention Is All You Need" in 2017, ChatGPT's public launch in November 2022) rather than any single proprietary source. Specific dates and figures are widely corroborated across academic and industry retrospectives.
Back to flowchartNext: Define Purpose & Scope
StartStage 1 of 8

1Define Purpose & Scope

Explain it like I'm 5

Imagine you're building a robot helper for a school project. Before you glue anything together, you decide what the robot will actually do — fetch your pencil, or answer homework questions? Skip this and you end up with a robot that tries to do everything and does nothing well.

The expert view

Scope definition is requirements engineering applied to a non-deterministic system. Because LLM agents fail unpredictably rather than crash predictably, an underspecified scope compounds into every downstream stage — prompt, tools, and evals all inherit its ambiguity. Treat it like a lightweight PRD: testable success criteria, explicit non-goals, and named constraints, signed off before any prompt is written.

Why

Skip this and every later decision — model, tools, memory — becomes guesswork with no way to tell if it worked.

What

A one-page brief: problem statement, target user, in-scope vs out-of-scope tasks, and 2-3 measurable success metrics.

When

Before writing a single prompt or line of code — revisited after every major round of user feedback.

How

Talk to 3-5 real users or stakeholders, write the brief in plain language, get one person to sign off before building starts.

In practice

In practice, most teams skip this step, or rush through it in five minutes, because it feels like paperwork standing between them and the fun part of building. That instinct is understandable but it's usually the single biggest reason agent projects fail quietly six weeks in: not because the model was bad, but because nobody agreed on what ‘working’ meant, so three different stakeholders quietly built three different mental models of what the agent was for.

A good scope document answers four questions a beginner can genuinely write down today, without needing any AI expertise at all. First, who is the user, specifically — not ‘customers’ but ‘a first-time customer trying to track a delayed order.’ Second, what is the one job the agent does — resist the urge to list five jobs; agents that try to do everything tend to do all five things averagely rather than one thing well. Third, what does success look like in a number you could actually measure. Fourth, what is explicitly out of scope, because agents don't know what they don't know, and an unscoped boundary is where most embarrassing failures happen.

None of this requires code. It requires the same discipline a product manager would bring to any new feature, applied to a system that behaves less predictably than ordinary software — which is exactly why it matters more here, not less.

Back: What is AI?Next: System Prompt Design
Stage 2 of 8

2System Prompt Design

Explain it like I'm 5

This is the rulebook you give your robot before it starts talking to people: 'be polite,' 'double-check your maths,' 'never share a password.' Without a rulebook, it might say the right thing one day and something totally wrong the next.

The expert view

The system prompt is the closest thing an LLM has to a constitution — the highest-priority context the model conditions on every turn. Effective system prompts separate persona (how it sounds), task instructions (what it does), and guardrails (what it refuses), and are versioned and tested like code rather than edited ad hoc in production.

Why

An agent without a clear system prompt is inconsistent — same question, different tone, different mistakes, every time.

What

A structured prompt covering goals, persona, step-by-step instructions, and explicit refusals/guardrails.

When

Drafted right after scope is locked, then iterated continuously as real conversations reveal gaps.

How

Start from a template, test against 10–20 realistic queries, tighten wording where the model drifts, version every change.

In practice

A system prompt is not a single magic paragraph you write once. In practice it is closer to a short internal policy document, and the best ones read that way: a sentence or two of role, a short numbered list of what to do, and an equally short list of what never to do. Beginners often write prompts that are too vague (‘be helpful and friendly’) or too long (a wall of edge cases the model never fully attends to). The middle ground that tends to work is specific but short.

One habit worth building early: treat every system prompt change the way a developer treats a code change — write down what you changed and why, and re-test against a small set of representative questions before it goes live. A fix for one conversation can easily break three others you didn't think to check. This is also where ‘guardrails’ earn their place — explicit instructions about what the agent must refuse are cheap to write and expensive to skip.

Back: Define Purpose & ScopeNext: Choose LLM
Stage 3 of 8

3Choose LLM

Explain it like I'm 5

Not all robot brains are the same. Some are super smart but slow and pricey, like a genius who thinks a long time. Some are fast and cheap but simpler. You pick the brain that fits the job — you don't need a genius to answer 'what's 2+2.'

The expert view

Model selection is a multi-objective optimisation across capability, latency, context window, and $/token — rarely dominated by one 'best' model. Production systems typically route: a cheap, fast model handles the bulk of simple turns, escalating to a frontier model only for genuinely hard reasoning, cutting spend 40–80% with negligible quality loss.

Why

The model is your biggest recurring cost and the ceiling on how well the agent can reason — get this wrong and you either overpay or underperform.

What

A primary model for hard tasks, and optionally a cheaper model for routing, classification, or simple turns.

When

After scope and prompt are drafted enough to test — re-evaluate whenever a materially better or cheaper model ships.

How

Prototype on 2–3 candidate models against the same test prompts; compare quality, latency, and cost per 1,000 real conversations.

In practice

For a beginner, the practical question isn't ‘which model is smartest’ — it's ‘which model is smart enough for this specific job, at a price I can sustain once this isn't a demo anymore.’ It's genuinely common for a well-scoped support or FAQ agent to run happily on a smaller, cheaper model, while a research or coding agent needs a frontier model's deeper reasoning. Testing two or three candidate models against the same twenty real questions tells you more in an afternoon than any benchmark leaderboard will.

Cost is also not just about the sticker price per million tokens. A cheaper model that needs three follow-up messages to get a task right can end up costing more, in both tokens and user patience, than a pricier model that gets it in one turn. And context window size matters more than it first appears: a model with a small context window will start silently forgetting the beginning of a long conversation, which shows up as the agent ‘getting worse’ the longer someone talks to it.

Back: System Prompt DesignNext: Tools & Integrations
Stage 4 of 8

4Tools & Integrations

Explain it like I'm 5

A brain alone can only think and talk — it can't check today's weather or send an email by itself. Tools are like giving your robot hands: now it can press buttons, open doors, and fetch things for you.

The expert view

Tool use converts a text predictor into an agent capable of taking real-world action. The key engineering decisions are which actions are exposed as callable functions, how tightly scoped each tool's permissions are, and whether orchestration happens via simple function calling or a standardised protocol like MCP that decouples tool servers from any one model.

Why

Without tools, the agent can only talk about doing things, not actually do them — a chatbot, not an agent.

What

A defined set of callable functions/APIs, each with a clear scope, input schema, and failure behaviour.

When

Added incrementally, starting with the 1–2 tools that unlock the core use case, expanded only as usage justifies it.

How

Wrap each external action as a small, well-documented function; test it in isolation before letting the agent call it autonomously.

In practice

The biggest mistake beginners make with tools is exposing too much, too soon. Every tool you give an agent is also a new way for it to make a mistake with real consequences — a ‘send email’ tool is genuinely useful, but if it's not scoped carefully, an agent can also send an email it shouldn't have, to someone it shouldn't have. The safer pattern is to start with read-only tools before granting write access, and to add a human-approval step for any action that's hard to undo.

It also helps to think of tools as a vocabulary you're teaching the agent, not a pile of capabilities you're bolting on. A tool with a clear name, a tightly defined input, and an honest description of what it does and doesn't do will get used correctly far more often than a vague, do-everything tool. This is also where the Model Context Protocol (MCP) is changing the landscape quickly: instead of a custom integration for every service, MCP gives tools a standard shape any compatible model can call.

Tools in the market today

Back: Choose LLMNext: Memory Systems
Stage 5 of 8

5Memory Systems

Explain it like I'm 5

Imagine talking to someone with no memory — every time you say hi, they've forgotten your name from two minutes ago! Memory lets your robot remember what you said earlier, what it learned, and where it put things.

The expert view

Agent memory spans a spectrum from ephemeral (conversation history in-context) to persistent (vector or relational stores queried at inference time). The core trade-off is retrieval precision vs. cost/latency: vector search trades exactness for semantic recall, structured stores trade flexibility for guaranteed accuracy — most production systems use both.

Why

Without memory, every interaction starts from zero — no personalisation, no learning, no long-running tasks.

What

Short-term context (the conversation itself), long-term recall (vector search over past interactions/docs), and structured facts (a normal database).

When

Add once the agent needs to reference something beyond the current message — most agents need conversation memory from day one.

How

Start with conversation history; add a vector store only once retrieval-augmented generation over documents is genuinely needed.

In practice

Memory is one of the most over-engineered parts of agent-building for beginners, largely because ‘vector database’ sounds like something every serious project needs on day one. In practice, most agents get most of their value from the simplest layer: the current conversation, kept in context. A vector database earns its complexity only once you have a genuine retrieval problem — a knowledge base or document library too large to fit in the model's context window.

When you do need it, the mental model is simpler than the terminology suggests: text gets converted into a list of numbers (an embedding) that captures its meaning, similar meanings end up as similar numbers, and a search becomes ‘find the stored chunks whose numbers are closest to my question's numbers.’ The practical trap to avoid is treating memory as infallible: retrieval can return the wrong chunk, so testing what the agent actually retrieves — not just what it eventually says — is worth doing before trusting it in production.

Back: Tools & IntegrationsNext: Orchestration
Stage 6 of 8

6Orchestration

Explain it like I'm 5

If your robot has many helpers — one checks weather, one sends texts, one does maths — orchestration is the manager deciding who does what, in what order, and what happens if someone messes up.

The expert view

Orchestration is the control plane: routing logic, state management across multi-step plans, and failure handling. As systems scale to multiple specialised sub-agents, this shifts from simple if/else routing to graph-based state machines (e.g. LangGraph) or queue-backed pipelines that can pause, retry, and resume individual steps independently.

Why

Complex tasks need more than one model call — orchestration is what turns isolated calls into a coherent multi-step process.

What

Routing rules, a way to trigger runs, state/parameters passed between steps, and explicit error handling for every external call.

When

Needed as soon as a task requires more than a single prompt-response turn, or involves more than one tool.

How

Map the task as a flowchart first, then implement it in a workflow tool or code — always wrap external calls with retry/fallback logic.

In practice

Orchestration is the part of an agent system that's genuinely invisible when it works and genuinely painful when it doesn't. For a single-step agent, you barely need orchestration at all. The moment a task needs two or more steps that depend on each other's results, you need something deciding the order, passing information between steps, and, critically, deciding what happens when step two fails after step one already succeeded.

That last part — failure handling — is the piece beginners skip most often, and it's the piece that determines whether your agent is a fun demo or something you can actually trust with real work. A good rule of thumb: for every tool call in your workflow, explicitly decide what the agent does if that call fails or times out, rather than assuming it will simply work.

Back: Memory SystemsNext: User Interface
Stage 7 of 8

7User Interface

Explain it like I'm 5

This is the part people actually see and touch — like the robot's face and voice. It could be a chat box, a website, or a message inside an app like Slack. If the brain and memory are the inside, the interface is the face.

The expert view

The interface is a distribution decision as much as a design one — it determines where the agent meets users, what latency/streaming constraints apply, and how much custom UI work is needed versus embedding in a surface (Slack, a website widget) users already trust.

Why

The best agent is worthless if it lives somewhere users never go — the interface decides adoption, not just aesthetics.

What

A conversational surface (chat widget, app, or bot) matched to where your users already are.

When

Designed in parallel with orchestration — interface constraints (streaming, voice, multi-turn) shape how the backend must respond.

How

Prototype on the cheapest channel that reaches real users first (often a website widget or Slack bot), then expand once the core flow is validated.

In practice

It's easy to treat the interface as the last, cosmetic step, but for most real agents it's closer to a distribution decision than a design one. An agent embedded inside Slack, where a team already spends its day, will get used far more than an objectively better agent living on a separate website nobody remembers to visit. Before investing in a custom interface, it's worth asking where your users already are, and meeting them there first.

The technical constraints of an interface also quietly shape what you can build behind it. A chat widget that streams responses token-by-token feels far more responsive than one that waits for the full answer, even if the total time is identical. Similarly, a voice interface imposes much stricter latency requirements than text, because silence on a phone call feels broken in a way a typing indicator does not.

Tools in the market today

Free / Freemium / Paid, with approximate India pricing (~₹96 = $1, July 2026).

ToolAccessPrice (approx, India)Best for
ChatbaseFreemiumFree (limited); Hobby $32–40/mo (~₹3,070–3,840); Standard $120–150/mo (~₹11,520–14,400); Pro $400–500/mo (~₹38,400–48,000)Fastest way to a website FAQ/support bot trained on your own content.

Custom build (React/Next.js + Claude or OpenAI API)FreeNo platform fee — you pay only for the API tokens usedFull control over UX; the right choice once a no-code tool's limits start to show.
Sources: Chatbase, Voiceflow, and Typebot pricing cross-checked across four independent 2026 comparison guides, April–May 2026. Prices are approximate and change frequently. Last verified: July 2026. Please check the vendor's official pricing page before purchase.
Back: OrchestrationNext: Testing & Evals
EndStage 8 of 8

8Testing & Evals

Explain it like I'm 5

Before you let your robot help real people, you test it — like a driving test before getting a licence. Does it give correct answers? Is it fast enough? Does it ever say something it shouldn't? After launch, you keep checking and improving it, forever.

The expert view

Evaluation for LLM agents spans three layers: unit tests on deterministic components (tool calls, parsers), LLM-as-judge regression suites for subjective output quality, and continuous production trace sampling to catch real-world drift that offline evals miss. Treating evals as CI gates — not a one-time pre-launch checklist — is what separates agents that stay reliable from ones that silently degrade.

Why

LLMs don't fail like normal software — the same prompt can succeed 95 times and fail the 96th. Without evals, you find out from angry users, not dashboards.

What

A test suite covering deterministic tool calls, a scored dataset of realistic prompts with expected outcomes, and live monitoring of production traffic.

When

Unit tests from day one; regression evals before every prompt or model change ships; production monitoring runs continuously.

How

Start with 20–50 hand-written test cases covering common and edge-case queries, automate scoring, and re-run on every change before deploy.

In practice

The single hardest thing to internalise about testing AI agents, if you're coming from traditional software, is that passing a test once proves almost nothing. A normal program either has a bug or it doesn't. An LLM agent can handle a tricky question correctly nine times and mishandle it the tenth, with no code having changed in between — because the model's output has an element of randomness by design, and tiny differences in phrasing can tip a borderline case either way.

A realistic starting point for a beginner is not a sophisticated evaluation pipeline — it's twenty to fifty real questions your users are likely to ask, including a handful of deliberately tricky ones, run every time you change the prompt or switch models, with someone actually reading the answers. Automated scoring and dedicated eval tools become worth the setup cost once you're iterating weekly; before that, a shared spreadsheet and five minutes of discipline before every deploy will catch most regressions.

Tools in the market today

Free / Freemium / Paid, with approximate India pricing (~₹96 = $1, July 2026).

ToolAccessPrice (approx, India)Best for
LangSmithFreemiumFree (5,000 traces/mo); Plus $39/seat/mo (~₹3,744) + usage-based trace overageDeepest integration if you're already building on LangChain/LangGraph.
BraintrustFreemiumFree Starter (10,000 scores/mo, $10 credit); Pro $249/mo (~₹23,900)Eval-first workflow — datasets, scorers, and experiment tracking for prompt regression testing.
LangfuseFree FreemiumFree, open-source, self-hosted (MIT licence); Cloud from ~$101/mo at 1M events (~₹9,700)Best open-source option if self-hosting is a requirement.

Back: User InterfaceNext: About the creator

Sujit Kulkarni

Creator of this guide · 15 Years in Investment Banking · AI Enthusiast & Eager Learner

Sujit has spent 15 years in the investment banking sector, working across a range of functions over that time. Alongside that, he's an enthusiastic, hands-on learner of AI — exploring Machine Learning, Deep Learning, and no-code/low-code tools out of genuine curiosity. He put this guide together to make agentic AI a little easier to understand for anyone starting out, the way he once did. He's a dedicated Claude enthusiast — and yes, he loves Claude.

15 yrs · Investment Banking AI Enthusiast Eager Learner No-Code / Low-Code Curious Claude Enthusiast 💚
